questions.title: JEE Advanced 2023 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
Let $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ be the lines $\vec{r}_1=\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$ and $\vec{r}_2=(\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+\hat{k})$, respectively. Let $X$ be the set of all the planes $H$ that contain the line $\ell_1$. For a plane $H$, let $d(H)$ denote the smallest possible distance between the points of $\ell_2$ and $H$. Let $H_0$ be a plane in $X$ for which $d\left(H_0\right)$ is the maximum value of $d(H)$ as $H$ varies over all planes in $X$.

Match each entry in List-I to the correct entries in List-II.

List - I List - II
(P) The value of $d\left(H_0\right)$ is (1) $\sqrt{3}$
(Q) The distance of the point $(0,1,2)$ from $H_0$ is (2) $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
(R) The distance of origin from $H_0$ is (3) 0
(S) The distance of origin from the point of intersection of planes $y=z, x=1$ and $H_0$ is (4) $\sqrt{2}$
(5) $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

The correct option is:
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & l_1: \vec{r}=\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \\\\ & l_2: \vec{r}=\hat{j}-\hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \\ & \end{aligned}$
For plane

$d(H)=$ Smallest possible distance between the points of $l_2$ and Plane.

$d\left(H_0\right)=$ Maximum value of $d(H)$

For $d\left(H_0\right)$



$l_2$ is Parallel to plane containing $l_1$

Equation of plane

$$ a(x)+b y+c z=0 $$

$\because$ It contain $\mathrm{l}_1$

$$ \therefore \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=0 $$ ..........(1)

For largest possible distance between plane (1) and $\mathrm{l}_2$ the line $\mathrm{l}_2$ must be parallel to plane (1)

$$ \therefore \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}=0 $$ .........(2)

By (1) and (2) $a=-c, b=0$

$\therefore$ Equation of plane $x-z=0$

(P) $ d\left(H_0\right)=P M=\left|\frac{0-(-1)}{\sqrt{1+1}}\right|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

(Q) Distance from $(0,1,2)$

$$ =\left|\frac{0-2}{\sqrt{2}}\right|=\sqrt{2} $$

(R) Distance from origin $(0,0,0)$

$$ =\left|\frac{0}{\sqrt{2}}\right|=0 $$

(S) Point of Intersection,

$$ x-z=0 $$

and

$$ \begin{aligned} & x=1, y=z \\\\ & \therefore x=1=z=y \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore$ Point of intersection $(1,1,1)$ Distance from origin

$$ =\sqrt{1^2+1^2+1^2}=\sqrt{3} $$

(a) Why is phenol more acidic than cyclohexanol?

(b) Give one chemical test to distinguish between the two.
questions.solution:

(b) When phenol react with Ferric Chloride, it gives violet colour but cyclohexanol remains colourless when react with $$\mathrm{FeCl}_3$$.

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question: A resistance $$R$$ and inductance $$L$$ and a capacitor $$C$$ all are connected in series with an $$\mathrm{AC}$$ supply. The resistance of $$R$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$ and for a given frequency, the inductive reactance of $$L$$ is 36 ohm and capacitive reactance of $$C$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$. If the current in the circuit is $$5 \mathrm{~amp}$$. Find the potential difference across $$R, L$$ and $$C$$.
questions.solution:
To determine the potential differences across the resistor $$R$$, inductor $$L$$, and capacitor $$C$$ in a series AC circuit, we can use Ohm's Law and the given impedances.

Given:

  • $$R = 24 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Inductive reactance $$X_L = 36 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Capacitive reactance $$X_C = 24 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Current in the circuit $$I = 5 \textrm{ amp}$$

First, we find the potential difference across each component using Ohm's Law:

  1. Potential difference across $$R$$:

$$ V_R = I \cdot R $$

$$ V_R = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_R = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$L$$:

$$ V_L = I \cdot X_L $$

$$ V_L = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 36 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_L = 180 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$C$$:

$$ V_C = I \cdot X_C $$

$$ V_C = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_C = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

Summarizing, the potential differences are:

  • $$V_R = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_L = 180 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_C = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: $$120, 180, 120 \textrm{ V}$$

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question: A direct current of $$10 \mathrm{~A}$$ is superimposed on an alternating current $$i=10 \sin \omega t$$ flowing through the wire. The effective value of the resulting current will be.
questions.solution:
The resulting current is the sum of the direct current and the alternating current:

$$I = I_{dc} + I_{ac} = 10 + 10 \sin \omega t$$

To find the effective value (also known as the root-mean-square or RMS value), we need to calculate the square root of the mean of the squared current:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} I^2 dt}$$

where T is the period of the alternating current.

Let's substitute the expression for I and simplify:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (10 + 10 \sin \omega t)^2 dt}$$

Expanding the square and integrating term by term:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (100 + 200 \sin \omega t + 100 \sin^2 \omega t) dt}$$

We know that the average value of $$ \sin \omega t $$ over a complete cycle is zero, and the average value of $$ \sin^2 \omega t $$ over a complete cycle is $$\frac{1}{2}$$. Therefore:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} (100T + 0 + 100 \cdot \frac{1}{2} T)} = \sqrt{150}$$

Simplifying further:

$$I_{rms} = 5 \sqrt{6} \mathrm{~A}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B: $$5 \sqrt{6} \mathrm{~A}$$.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2023
questions.question:
An alternating voltage of $$220 \mathrm{~V}$$ is applied across a device $$X$$. A current of 0.22 A flows in the circuit and it lags behind the applied voltage in phase by $$\pi / 2$$ radian. When the same voltage is applied across another device $$Y$$, the current in the circuit remains the same and it is in phase with the applied voltage.

(i) Name the devices $$X$$ and $$Y$$ and,

(ii) Calculate the current flowing in the circuit when the same voltage is applied across the series combination of $$\mathrm{X}$$ and $$\mathrm{Y}$$.
questions.solution:
(i) Since, current lags behind the voltage, $$X$$ is an inductor.

Since, current and voltage remain in phase, $$\mathrm{Y}$$ is a resistor.

Since,

$$\begin{aligned} X_L & =\frac{V}{I}=\frac{220}{0.22} \\ & =1000 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

And,

$$\begin{aligned} R & =\frac{220}{0.22} \\ & =1000 \Omega \\ Z & =\sqrt{R^2+X_L^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(1000)^2+(1000)^2} \\ & =1000 \sqrt{2} \Omega \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { So, the required current }=\frac{220}{Z}=\frac{220}{1000 \sqrt{2}}=0.155 \mathrm{~A}$$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2023
questions.question: State the basic principle behind the working of an ac generator. Briefly describe its working and obtain the expression for the instantaneous value of emf induced.
questions.solution:
Basic principle of working of AC generator: Basic principle of working of AC generator is electromagnetic induction. A copper coil known a armature is rotated in a strong magnetic field and emf is induced in the coil according to the Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction and the direction of induced emf is determined by Fleming's right hand rule.

Working of ac generator:

Main components of ac generator are: (i) Armature, (ii) Field magnet, (iii) slip ring (iv) Brush Armature (ABCD) is a copper coil wound on a soft iron core. The armature is rotated by a turbine. The armature is placed in between poles of a strong permanent magnet (NS) known as field magnet. Two ends of armature coil are connected to the slip rings(R$$_1$$ and R$$_2$$). Carbon brushes (B_$$_1$$ and B$$_1$$) kept just in firm contact with the rings. External circuit is connected with the brushes.

When armature rotates in the magnetic field induced emf is generated which is supplied to the external circuit through the brushes.

Expression of instantaneous emf induced:

If the armature has $$\mathrm{N}$$ number of turns, then magnetic flux through the coil is

$$\begin{aligned} \phi & =N(\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}) \\ & =N B A \cos \theta \end{aligned}$$

If $$\omega$$ is the angular velocity, then emf induced

$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon & =-\frac{d \phi}{d t} \\ & =\mathrm{NBA} \omega \sin \omega \mathrm{t} \\ \therefore\quad \varepsilon \quad & =\varepsilon_0 \sin \omega \mathrm{t} \quad\left(\text { where } \mathrm{NBA} \omega=\varepsilon_0\right) \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Outside Delhi Set 1 - 2023
questions.question: A series CR circuit with $$R=200 \Omega$$ and $$C=(50 / \pi)$$ $$\mu \mathrm{F}$$ is connected across an ac source of peak voltage $$\varepsilon_0=100 \mathrm{~V}$$ and frequency $$v=50 \mathrm{~Hz}$$. Calculate (a) impedance of the circuit $$(\mathrm{Z})$$, (b) phase angle $$(\phi)$$, and (c) voltage across the resistor.
questions.solution:
$$\text { (a) Given, } R=200 \Omega, C=\frac{50}{\pi} \mu F$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { Impedance: } & Z=\sqrt{R^2+\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2} \\ \text { Or, } & Z=\sqrt{(200)^2+\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times \frac{50}{\pi} \times 10^{-6}}\right)^2} \\ \text { Or, } & Z=\sqrt{(200)^2+(200)^2} \\ \therefore & Z=200 \sqrt{2} \end{array}$$

(b) Phase angle:

$$\begin{aligned} \theta & =\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{\omega C R} \\ \text { or, } \quad \theta & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times \frac{50}{\pi} \times 10^{-6} \times 200}\right) \end{aligned}$$

or, $$\theta=\tan ^{-1} 1$$

$$\therefore \quad \theta=45^0$$

(c) Voltage across resistor:

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & I_{R M S} =\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{RMS}}}{\mathrm{Z}} \\ \text { Or, } & I_{R M S} =\frac{\frac{\varepsilon_0}{\sqrt{2}}}{\mathrm{Z}} \\ \text { Or, } & I_{R M S} =\frac{\frac{100}{\sqrt{2}}}{200 \sqrt{2}} \\ \therefore & I_{R M S} =\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~A} \\ & V_R =I_{R M S} \times R \\ \text { Or, } & V_R =\frac{1}{4} \times 200 \\ \therefore & \quad V_R =50 \mathrm{~V} \end{array}$$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2023 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
A series LCR circuit is connected to a $45 \sin (\omega t)$ Volt source. The resonant angular frequency of the circuit is $10^5 ~\mathrm{rad}~ \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ and current amplitude at resonance is $I_0$. When the angular frequency of the source is $\omega=8 \times 10^4 ~\mathrm{rad} ~\mathrm{s}^{-1}$, the current amplitude in the circuit is $0.05 I_0$. If $L=50 ~\mathrm{mH}$, match each entry in List-I with an appropriate value from List-II and choose the correct option.

List - I List - II
(P) $I_0$ in $\mathrm{mA}$ (1) 44.4
(Q) The quality factor of the circuit (2) 18
(R) The bandwidth of the circuit in $\mathrm{rad}~ \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ (3) 400
(S) The peak power dissipated at resonance in Watt (4) 2250
(5) 500
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \omega_0=10^5 \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}, L=50 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H} \\\\ & \Rightarrow X_L=5000 \Omega=X_C \text { (at resonance) } \\\\ & \frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}=\omega_0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow L_C=\frac{1}{\omega_0^2} \\\\ & \Rightarrow C=2 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~F} \\\\ & I_0=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}} \\\\ & I_0=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{R} ..........(i) \end{aligned}$

$$ \begin{aligned} 0.05 I_0 & =\frac{\varepsilon_0}{\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}} ..........(ii) \\\\ \text { At } \omega & =8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1} \\\\ X_L & =8 \times 10^4 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}=4000 \Omega \\\\ X_C & =\frac{1}{\omega_C}=6250 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2} =\frac{R}{0.05} \\\\ & R^2+(6250-4000)^2 =400 R^2 \\\\ & \Rightarrow R^2 \simeq \frac{(2250)^2}{400} \\\\ & R =\frac{2250}{20}=112.5 \Omega \\\\ &I_0 =\frac{\varepsilon_0}{R}=\frac{45}{112.5} \\\\ & =400 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

$(\mathrm{P} \rightarrow 3)$

$\begin{aligned} \rightarrow Q & =\frac{1}{R} \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \\\\ \Rightarrow Q & =\frac{2}{2250} \times \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-7}}}=\frac{10^4}{225} \\\\ & =44.4\\\\ (\mathrm{Q} \rightarrow 1) \\\\ Q & =\frac{\omega_0}{B W}\end{aligned}$

$\Rightarrow B W=\frac{\omega_0}{Q}=\frac{10^5}{\frac{10^4}{225}}=2250$

$(\mathrm{R} \rightarrow 4)$

$\begin{array}{rrr}P =\frac{\varepsilon_0^2}{R} \text { (peak power) } \\\\ =\frac{45^2}{112.5}=18 \mathrm{~W} \end{array}$

$(S \rightarrow 2)$

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question:
Select the word-pair in which the two words are related in the same way as are the two words in the following word-pair.

Growth : Expansion
options:
Child : Birth
Urge : Request
First : Last
Temporary : Permanent
questions.solution:
To understand the relationship between "Growth" and "Expansion," let's break down the pair. "Growth" refers to the process of increasing in size, quantity, or significance, while "Expansion" refers to the process of something becoming larger or more extensive. Therefore, the relationship can be summarized as a cause and effect or state that follows from an initial state.

Now, let's evaluate each option to identify which one shares a similar relationship:

Option A: Child : Birth

The relationship here can be described as the origin or commencement. A "Child" is the result or outcome of "Birth." This relationship, however, is not exactly similar to growth leading to expansion but rather an origin-result relationship.

Option B: Urge : Request

This relationship describes a situation where an "Urge" (a strong desire or impulse) can lead to a "Request" (an act of asking for something). This is quite similar to the cause-effect relationship seen in Growth leading to Expansion.

Option C: First : Last

This relationship is one of sequential order, where "First" and "Last" represent the beginning and ending points in a series. This does not match the growth-expansion relationship, which is transformational rather than positional.

Option D: Temporary : Permanent

This relationship is one of contrast, where "Temporary" means lasting for a limited period and "Permanent" means lasting indefinitely. This does not fit the pattern of growth leading to expansion.

Given the options, the correct word-pair that shares a similar relationship to "Growth" and "Expansion" is:

Option B: Urge : Request

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question: Flourine is related to Halogen in the same way as Helium is related to _________.
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option A: inert gas. Here's why:

The question is asking about the relationship between elements based on their position in the periodic table.

Flourine (F) is a member of the halogen group (Group 17). Halogens are highly reactive nonmetals.

Helium (He) is a member of the noble gas group (Group 18). Noble gases are known for being extremely unreactive, hence the name "inert gases."

So, the relationship is that both Flourine and Helium are in the same column of the periodic table, indicating they share similar chemical properties.

Let's look at why the other options are incorrect:

Option B: Gas: While both Fluorine and Helium are gases at room temperature, this is not the specific relationship the question is looking for. Many elements are gases.

Option C: Radioactive: Neither Fluorine nor Helium are inherently radioactive. Some isotopes of these elements may be radioactive, but that's not the primary characteristic defining their groups.

Option D: Metal: Both Fluorine and Helium are nonmetals.

The parental support can fortify a child's learning.

Option A: weaken - This means to make weaker or less strong. This is the opposite of "fortify".

Option B: encourage - This means to give support, confidence, or hope to someone. This is not the opposite of "fortify."

Option C: brace - This means to prepare for something difficult or to support. This is similar to "fortify."

Option D: strengthen - This means to make something stronger. This is synonymous with "fortify."

The option that is opposite in meaning to "fortify" is:

The underlined word in the sentence is commotion. To find the opposite in meaning, we first need to understand what "commotion" means. Commotion refers to a state of confused and noisy disturbance.

Now, let's analyze the options:

Option A: quietude

Quietude means a state of stillness, calmness, and tranquility. This is the opposite of commotion.

Option B: uproar

Uproar is very similar in meaning to commotion, as it refers to a loud and impassioned noise or disturbance.

Option C: tempest

Tempest means a violent storm, typically one with high winds and rain, which is related to a disturbance or commotion.

Option D: stir

Stir indicates a slight movement or disturbance, which is somewhat similar to commotion but less intense.

From the analysis, it is clear that the word quietude is the opposite in meaning of commotion. Hence, the correct option is:

Option A: quietude

They are making plans for the abatement of the nuisance within a specified time.
questions.solution:
The word "abatement" in the sentence means the reduction, decrease, or diminishing of something, especially something perceived as a nuisance or a negative factor. To select the option that is opposite in meaning to "abatement," let's analyze the options given:

Option A: subsidence
Subsidence means the gradual caving in or sinking of an area of land. This word is related to reduction or decrease in ground level, which is similar in concept to abatement. Therefore, this is not the opposite of abatement.

Option B: ebbing
Ebbing means the movement of the tide out to sea, or a gradual decline or diminishment of something. This is also synonymous with reduction or decrease, hence it is not the opposite of abatement.

Option C: accumulation
Accumulation means the gathering or increase of something over time. This is directly opposite to abatement, as it refers to an increase rather than a reduction. Therefore, this is a strong candidate for the opposite of abatement.

Option D: mitigation
Mitigation means the process of making something less severe, serious, or painful. This again implies a form of reduction or lessening and is not opposite to abatement.

Based on the analysis, the option opposite in meaning to abatement is:

Option C: accumulation

Ignorance is the lack of knowledge, education, or awareness. This is the opposite of sagacity.

Sapience is similar to wisdom and sagacity, implying keen perception and sound judgment. This is not the opposite.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option B: modest. Here's why:

The word "ostentatious" means characterized by vulgar or pretentious display; showy. It implies an excessive desire to attract attention or impress others.

"Modest" is the opposite of ostentatious. It means humble, unpretentious, and not showy.

Let's look at the other options:

  • Option A: tawdry - This means cheap and showy, similar to ostentatious.
  • Option C: gaudy - This means excessively bright or showy, also similar to ostentatious.
  • Option D: apparent - This means obvious or clearly seen. While it's not a direct synonym for ostentatious, it doesn't convey the opposite meaning.

Therefore, the word "modest" is the only word that accurately reflects the opposite meaning of "ostentatious".

The experience of Covid Pandemic was excruciating for much of the population.
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option D: mild. Here's why:

The word "excruciating" means extremely painful or agonizing. It describes something that causes intense suffering or distress.

Let's look at the options:

Option A: gnawing - Gnawing suggests a persistent, often uncomfortable feeling that lingers. While it can be unpleasant, it doesn't necessarily imply the same level of intense pain as "excruciating."

Option B: gruelling - Grueling means extremely tiring or demanding. While it describes something difficult, it doesn't carry the same sense of intense pain as "excruciating."

Option C: harrowing - Harrowing means extremely distressing or frightening. It's similar to "excruciating" in that it describes something causing intense negative emotions, but it doesn't have the specific connotation of physical pain.

Option D: mild - Mild means gentle, not severe or intense. This is the direct opposite of "excruciating." If something is mild, it is not painful or distressing.

Therefore, the word "mild" is the only option that accurately expresses the opposite meaning of "excruciating."

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question: A cylindrical tank of radius $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$ is being filled with wheat at the rate of $$200 \pi$$ cubic metre per hour. Then, the depth of the wheat is increasing at the rate of
questions.solution:
Let $$r$$ be the radius of the cylinder.

Then, volume $$(V)$$ of the cylinder is given by,

$$\begin{aligned} & V=\pi(\text { radius })^2 \times \text { height } \\ & \Rightarrow V=\pi(10)^2 h \\ & \Rightarrow V=100 \pi h \end{aligned}$$

On differentiating w.r.t. $$t$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d V}{d t}=100 \pi \frac{d h}{d t} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 200 \pi=100 \pi \frac{d h}{d t} \\ & {\left[\because \text { Given, } \frac{d V}{d t}=200 \pi \mathrm{m}^3 / \mathrm{h}\right]} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{200 \pi}{100 \pi}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{h} \\ \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question: Water is being filled at the rate of $$1 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{s}$$ in a right circular conical vessel (vertex downwards) of height $$35 \mathrm{~cm}$$ and diameter $$14 \mathrm{~cm}$$. When the height of the water levels is $$10 \mathrm{~cm}$$, the rate (in $$\mathrm{cm}^2 / \mathrm{sec}$$) at which the wet conical surface area of the vessel increases is
questions.solution:
Let $$r$$ be the radius and $$h$$ be the height of cone.

$$\therefore \quad \frac{r}{h}=\frac{7}{35}=\frac{1}{5} \Rightarrow r=\frac{h}{5} \mathrm{~cm}$$

Now, volume of cone $$=V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h=\frac{1}{75} \pi h^3$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d \dot{V}}{d t}=\frac{1}{75} \pi\left(3 h^2\right) \frac{d h}{d t}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{1 \times 75}{\pi \times 3 h^2}$$

$$ \left[\because \text { Given, } \frac{d V}{d t}=1 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{sec}\right] $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } A=\pi r l=\pi r \sqrt{r^2+h^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=\pi r^2 \sqrt{1+\frac{h^2}{r^2}}=\frac{\pi h^2}{25} \sqrt{1+25} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{25} \pi h^2 \\ & \therefore \frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{25} \pi \cdot 2 h \frac{d h}{d t} \\ & \left.\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d A}{d t}\right|_{h=10}=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{25} \pi \times 2 \times 10 \times \frac{1 \times 75}{\pi \times 3 \times 10^2} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{26}}{5} \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Mathematics Delhi Set 1 - 2023
questions.question: The derivative of $$x^{2 x}$$ w.r.t. $$x$$ is:
questions.solution:
To find the derivative of $$x^{2 x}$$ with respect to $$x$$, we use the logarithmic differentiation technique due to the variable exponents. Let's define the function as $$y = x^{2x}$$ and then differentiate both sides with respect to $$x$$.

First, we take the natural logarithm of both sides to simplify differentiation:

$$\ln{y} = \ln{(x^{2x})}$$

Using properties of logarithms, we can simplify the right hand side:

$$\ln{y} = 2x\ln{x}$$

Next, differentiate both sides with respect to $$x$$:

$$\frac{1}{y}\frac{dy}{dx} = 2\ln{x} + 2x\cdot\frac{1}{x}$$

Simplifying gives us:

$$\frac{1}{y}\frac{dy}{dx} = 2\ln{x} + 2$$

Remember that $$y = x^{2x}$$, so substituting back gives us:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = x^{2x}\left(2\ln{x} + 2\right)$$

Therefore, the derivative of $$x^{2x}$$ with respect to $$x$$ is $$2x^{2x}(1 + \log{x})$$.

Thus, the correct answer is Option C: $$2x^{2x}(1+\log{x})$$.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Mathematics Delhi Set 1 - 2023
questions.question: The interval in which the function $$f(x)=2 x^3+9 x^2 +12 x-1$$ is decreasing is :
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} f(x) & =2 x^3+9 x^2+12 x-1 \\\\ f^{\prime}(x) & =6 x^2+18 x+12-0 \end{aligned}$$

for decreasing function $$f^{\prime}(x)<0$$

$$\begin{aligned} 6\left(x^2+3 x+2\right) & <0 \\\\ 6(x+2)(x+1) & <0 \end{aligned}$$

$$f(x) \text { is decreasing in interval }(-2,-1)$$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Mathematics Delhi Set 1 - 2023
questions.question:
(a) The median of an equilateral triangle is increasing at the rate of $$2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$$. Find the rate at which its side is increasing.

OR

(b) Sum of two numbers is 5. If the sum of the cubes of these numbers is least, then find the sum of the squares of these numbers.
questions.solution:
Let the length of the median be x cm.

and side of equilateral triangle be $$y \mathrm{~cm}$$

In $$\triangle \mathrm{ABD}$$

$$\begin{aligned} A B^2 & =A D^2+B D^2\left(\angle D=90^{\circ}\right) \\\\ y^2 & =x^2+\left(\frac{y}{2}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{3}{4} y^2 & =x^2 \\\\ y^2 & =\frac{4}{3} x^2 \\\\ y & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} x \\\\ \frac{d}{d t}(y) & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \frac{d}{d t}(x) \\\\ \frac{d y}{d t} & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{d x}{d t} \\\\ \frac{d y}{d t} & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times 2 \sqrt{3}\\\\ &=4 ~\text{cm/s} \end{aligned}$$

Hence side of equilateral triangle be increase at $$4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$$

OR

(b) Let the number be $$x$$ and $$y$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad x+y & =5 \quad \text{.... (1)}\\\\ S & =x^3+y^3 \quad \text{.... (2)} \end{aligned}$$

from (1) & (2)

$$\begin{aligned} S & =x^3+(5-x)^3 \\ \frac{d S}{d x} & =3 x^2-3(5-x)^2 \\\\ \frac{d S}{d x} & =3 x^2-75+30 x-3 x^2 \\\\ & =-75+30 x \end{aligned}$$

for maximum and minimum

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d S}{d x} & =0 \\ -75+30 x & =0 \\ x & =\frac{75}{30}=\frac{5}{2} \\\\ \frac{d S}{d x} & =-75+30 x \\\\ \frac{d^2 S}{d x^2} & =30 \\\\ \frac{d^2 S}{d x^2} & >0 \end{aligned}$$

Hence $$S$$ is minimum at $$x=\frac{5}{2}$$

Minimum value of $$x^2+y^2$$

$$=\frac{25}{4}+\frac{25}{4}=\frac{25}{2}$$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2023 Paper 1 Online
questions.question: Let $Q$ be the cube with the set of vertices $\left\{\left(x_1, x_2, x_3\right) \in \mathbb{R}^3: x_1, x_2, x_3 \in\{0,1\}\right\}$. Let $F$ be the set of all twelve lines containing the diagonals of the six faces of the cube $Q$. Let $S$ be the set of all four lines containing the main diagonals of the cube $Q$; for instance, the line passing through the vertices $(0,0,0)$ and $(1,1,1)$ is in $S$. For lines $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$, let $d\left(\ell_1, \ell_2\right)$ denote the shortest distance between them. Then the maximum value of $d\left(\ell_1, \ell_2\right)$, as $\ell_1$ varies over $F$ and $\ell_2$ varies over $S$, is :
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{DR}^{\prime} \mathrm{S}$ of $\mathrm{OG}=1,1,1$

DR'S of AF $=-1,1,1$

DR'S of CE = 1, 1, -1

$\mathrm{DR}^{\prime} \mathrm{S}$ of $\mathrm{BD}=1,-1,1$

Equation of $\mathrm{OG} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}$

Equation of $\mathrm{AB} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{0}$

Normal to both the line's

$$ =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right|=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k} $$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}$

S.D. $=\frac{|\hat{\mathrm{i}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})|}{|\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 16 April 2023
questions.question:
Consider the following statements :

1. f(x) = In x is increasing in (0, ∞)

2. $g(x)=e^x+e^{\frac{1}{x}} $ is decreasing in (0, ∞)

Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?
questions.solution:
To determine which of the statements are correct, let's analyze each one in detail:

Statement 1: $ f(x) = \ln x $ is increasing in $ (0, \infty) $

To verify if $ f(x) = \ln x $ is increasing, we need to find the derivative of $ f(x) $ and check its sign in the interval $ (0, \infty) $.

The derivative of $ f(x) = \ln x $ is given by:

$$ f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx} (\ln x) = \frac{1}{x} $$

In the interval $ (0, \infty) $, $ x $ is always positive, therefore $ \frac{1}{x} $ is always positive. Hence, $ f(x) = \ln x $ is an increasing function in $ (0, \infty) $.

Thus, Statement 1 is correct.

Statement 2: $ g(x) = e^x + e^{\frac{1}{x}} $ is decreasing in $ (0, \infty) $

To verify if $ g(x) = e^x + e^{\frac{1}{x}} $ is decreasing, we need to find the derivative of $ g(x) $ and check its sign in the interval $ (0, \infty) $.

The derivative of $ g(x) $ is given by:

$$ g'(x) = \frac{d}{dx} \left( e^x + e^{\frac{1}{x}} \right) $$

Applying the chain rule to the second term, we get:

$$ g'(x) = e^x + e^{\frac{1}{x}} \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{x^2} \right) = e^x - \frac{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}{x^2} $$

To determine if $ g'(x) $ is negative in $ (0, \infty) $, we need to check if:

$$ e^x - \frac{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}{x^2} < 0 $$

However, for $ x > 0 $, $ e^x $ grows exponentially, and $ e^{\frac{1}{x}} $ is always positive. Thus, $ e^x $ will always be larger than $ \frac{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}{x^2} $ for $ x > 1 $. This indicates that $ g'(x) $ is generally positive in $ (0, \infty) $.

Therefore, $ g(x) = e^x + e^{\frac{1}{x}} $ is not a decreasing function in $ (0, \infty) $.

Thus, Statement 2 is incorrect.

Conclusion:

The correct answer is:

Option A: 1 only

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 16 April 2023
questions.question: What is the derivative of sin<sup>2</sup> x with respect to cos<sup>2</sup>x ?
questions.solution:
To find the derivative of $$\sin^2 x$$ with respect to $$\cos^2 x$$, we need to use the concept of implicit differentiation. We will first express the derivatives of these functions with respect to $$x$$ and then use the chain rule.

First, let's find the derivatives of $$\sin^2 x$$ and $$\cos^2 x$$ with respect to $$x$$:

The derivative of $$\sin^2 x$$ with respect to $$x$$ is found using the chain rule:

$$\frac{d}{dx} (\sin^2 x) = 2 \sin x \cdot \cos x = \sin 2x$$

The derivative of $$\cos^2 x$$ with respect to $$x$$ is also found using the chain rule:

$$\frac{d}{dx} (\cos^2 x) = 2 \cos x \cdot (-\sin x) = -\sin 2x$$

Now we need to find the derivative of $$\sin^2 x$$ with respect to $$\cos^2 x$$. By using the chain rule in the form:

$$\frac{d}{d(\cos^2 x)} (\sin^2 x) = \frac{\frac{d}{dx} (\sin^2 x)}{\frac{d}{dx} (\cos^2 x)}$$

Substituting the derivatives we calculated earlier, we get:

$$\frac{d}{d(\cos^2 x)} (\sin^2 x) = \frac{\sin 2x}{-\sin 2x} = -1$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A: -1.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 3 September 2023
questions.question: What is the maximum value of y?
questions.solution:
Minima and Maxima of a Function: These represent the points where the first derivative (slope) of the function equals zero.

To determine the minima or maxima of a function $ f(x) $, follow these steps:

Step 1: Calculate the first derivative $ f'(x) $ of the function.

Step 2: Set the first derivative equal to zero and solve for the critical points (e.g., $ x_1 $, $ x_2 $).

Step 3: Find the second derivative $ f''(x) $ and evaluate its sign at the critical points.

Step 4: If $ f''(x) < 0 $, the function has a maximum at that point; if $ f''(x) > 0 $, it has a minimum at that point.

Calculation:

Given: $ 4x^2 + y^2 = 9 $

To find the maximum $ y $:

$ y = \sqrt{9 - 4x^2} $

Differentiate with respect to $ x $:

$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{-8x}{2 \sqrt{9 - 4x^2}} $

Set the derivative equal to zero to find the critical points:

$ \frac{-8x}{2 \sqrt{9 - 4x^2}} = 0 $

This implies:

$ x = 0 $

To confirm $ y $ is a maximum at $ x = 0 $, compute the second derivative:

$ \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{-4\sqrt{9 - 4x^2} - 32x^2 / (2 \sqrt{9 - 4x^2})}{(9 - 4x^2)} $

At $ x = 0 $:

$ \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{-4 \times 3}{9^{3/2}} = -\frac{4}{3} $

Since $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} < 0$, $ y $ has a maximum at $ x = 0 $.

Thus, the maximum value of $ y $ is:

$ y = \sqrt{9 - 4(0)^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3 $

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 3 September 2023
questions.question: What is the maximum value of xy ?
questions.solution:
To find the maximum value of $ xy $ given the equation $ 4x^2 + y^2 = 9 $, Lagrange multipliers can be used. Define the function $ f(x,y) = xy $ and the constraint $ g(x,y) = 4x^2 + y^2 - 9 = 0 $.

Using Lagrange multipliers:

Set up the Lagrangian: $$ \mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = xy + \lambda (9 - 4x^2 - y^2) $$

Take the partial derivatives with respect to $ x $, $ y $, and $ \lambda $: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = y - 8x\lambda = 0 $$ $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = x - 2y\lambda = 0 $$ $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = 9 - 4x^2 - y^2 = 0 $$

Solve the system of equations: From $ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 0 $: $$ y = 8x\lambda $$

From $ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 0 $: $$ x = 2y\lambda $$

Substitute $ y = 8x\lambda $ into $ x = 2y\lambda $: $$ x = 2(8x\lambda)\lambda $$ $$ x = 16x\lambda^2 $$ $$ 1 = 16\lambda^2 $$ $$ \lambda^2 = \frac{1}{16} $$ $$ \lambda = \pm \frac{1}{4} $$

Consider $ \lambda = \frac{1}{4} $: $$ y = 8x\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = 2x $$

Substitute $ y = 2x $ into the constraint $ 4x^2 + y^2 = 9 $: $$ 4x^2 + (2x)^2 = 9 $$ $$ 4x^2 + 4x^2 = 9 $$ $$ 8x^2 = 9 $$ $$ x^2 = \frac{9}{8} $$ $$ x = \pm \frac{3}{2\sqrt{2}} = \pm \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{4} $$

For $ x = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{4} $: $$ y = 2 \left(\frac{3\sqrt{2}}{4}\right) = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{2} $$

Now calculate $ xy $: $$ xy = \left(\frac{3\sqrt{2}}{4}\right) \left(\frac{3\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) = \frac{9 \cdot 2}{8} = \frac{18}{8} = \frac{9}{4} $$

Therefore, the maximum value of $ xy $ is $ \boxed{\frac{9}{4}} $.

questions.solution:

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Required Area } & =\int_2^4 x d y=\int_2^4 \frac{8-y}{2} d y \\\\ & =\left[4 y-\frac{y^2}{4}\right]_2^4 \\\\ & =[16-4-8+1] \\\\ \text { Required area } & =5 \text { unit }^2 \end{aligned}$$

Find the area of the following region using integration:

$$\left\{(x, y): y^2 \leq 2 x \text { and } y \geq x-4\right\}$$
questions.solution:
Given:

$$\begin{aligned} y^2 & =2 x \quad \text{.... (i)}\\\\ y & =x-4 \quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Required area is OABCO

From (1) and (2)

$$\begin{aligned} &(x-4)^2=2 x \\\\ & x^2-10 x+16=0 \\\\ &(x-8)(x-2)=0 \\\\ & x=8 \text { and } x=2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \text { Intersection points }(2,-2) \text { and }(8,4)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Required Area } & =\left[\frac{y^2}{2}+4 y-\frac{y^3}{6}\right]_{-2}^4 \\\\ & =\left(8+16-\frac{32}{3}-2+8-\frac{4}{3}\right) \\\\ & =30-12 \\\\ & =18 \text { unit }^2 \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2023 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
Let $n \geq 2$ be a natural number and $f:[0,1] \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be the function defined by

$$ f(x)= \begin{cases}n(1-2 n x) & \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq \frac{1}{2 n} \\\\ 2 n(2 n x-1) & \text { if } \frac{1}{2 n} \leq x \leq \frac{3}{4 n} \\\\ 4 n(1-n x) & \text { if } \frac{3}{4 n} \leq x \leq \frac{1}{n} \\\\ \frac{n}{n-1}(n x-1) & \text { if } \frac{1}{n} \leq x \leq 1\end{cases} $$

If $n$ is such that the area of the region bounded by the curves $x=0, x=1, y=0$ and $y=f(x)$ is 4 , then the maximum value of the function $f$ is :
questions.solution:
$f(x)$ is decreasing in $\left[0, \frac{1}{2 n}\right]$

increasing in $\left[\frac{1}{2 n}, \frac{3}{4 n}\right]$

decreasing in $\left[\frac{3}{4 n}, \frac{1}{n}\right]$

increasing in $\left[\frac{1}{n}, 1\right]$



$\begin{aligned} \text { Area } & =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2 n} \times n+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2 n} \times n+\frac{1}{2} \times\left(1-\frac{1}{n}\right) \times n \\\\ 4 & =\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{n-1}{2} \\\\ 4 & =\frac{1}{2}+\frac{n-1}{2} \\\\ 4 & =\frac{n}{2} \\\\ n & =8\end{aligned}$

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question: The area of the region bounded by the parabola $$y=x^2+1$$ and lines $$y=x+1, y=0, x=\frac{1}{2}$$ and $$x=2$$ is
questions.solution:
The described region is :

It is clear that

$$\begin{aligned} & A=\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0\right), B=(2,0), C=(2,3), D=(1,2) \\ & E=\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right) \text { and } F=\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{5}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Required area $$=$$ Area of region A B C D E A $$-$$ Area of region EDFE

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3}{2}+3\right)\left(2-\frac{1}{2}\right)-\int_\limits{1 / 2}^1\left[(x+1)-\left(x^2+1\right)\right] d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{9}{2} \times \frac{3}{2}-\int_\limits{1 / 2}^1\left(x-x^2\right) d x \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{1 / 2}^1 \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\left[\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right)-\left(\frac{1}{8}-\frac{1}{24}\right)\right] \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\left(\frac{1}{6}-\frac{1}{12}\right) \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\frac{1}{12}=\frac{79}{24} \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question: Let the functions $$f: R \rightarrow R$$ and $$g: R \rightarrow R$$ be defined by $$f(x)=e^{x-1}-e^{-|x-1|}$$ and $$g(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right)$$. Then, the area of the region in the first quadrant bounded by the curves $$y=f(x), y=g(x)$ and $x=0$$ is.
questions.solution:
Given, $$f(x)=e^x-e^{-|x-1|}$$

$$\therefore \quad f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}0 & \text { if } x < 1 \\ e^{x-1}-e^{1-x} & , \text { if } x \geq 1\end{array}\right\}$$

The graph of $$f$$ and $$g$$ can be plotted in the first quadrant as given below.

The point of intersection, $$A$$ is given by $$f(x)=g(x)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad e^{x-1}-e^{1-x}=\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad e^{x-1}=3 e^{1-x} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(e^{x-1}\right)^2=3 \Rightarrow e^{2 x-2}=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 x-2=\ln 3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=1+\frac{1}{2} \ln 3 \\ & \therefore \text { Required area }=\int_\limits0^1\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right) d x +\int_\limits1^{1+\frac{1}{2} \ln 3}\left[\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right)-\left(e^{x-1}-e^{1-x}\right)\right] d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left[e^{x-1}-e^{1-x}\right]_0^1-\left[\frac{1}{2} e^{x-1}+\frac{3}{2} e^{1-x}\right]_1^{1+\frac{1}{2} \ln 3} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(0-\frac{1}{e}+e\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left[e^{\ln \sqrt{3}}+3 e^{-\ln \sqrt{3}}-4\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(e-\frac{1}{e}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{3}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{3}}-4\right) \\ & =-\frac{1}{2}(2 \sqrt{3}-4)+\frac{1}{2}\left(e-\frac{1}{e}\right) \\ & =(2-\sqrt{3})+\frac{1}{2}\left(e-\frac{1}{e}\right) \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 16 April 2023
questions.question: For what value of m with m < 0, is the area bounded by the lines y = x, y = mx and x = 2 equal to 3 ?
questions.solution:
To determine the value of $$m$$ for which the area bounded by the lines $$y = x$$, $$y = mx$$, and $$x = 2$$ is equal to 3, we need to find the area of the triangle formed by these lines. The vertices of the triangle are the points of intersection of these lines.

The lines intersect at the following points:

  • $$y = x$$ and $$x = 2$$ intersect at the point $$(2, 2)$$.
  • $$y = mx$$ and $$x = 2$$ intersect at the point $$(2, 2m)$$.
  • The lines $$y = x$$ and $$y = mx$$ intersect at the origin $$(0, 0)$$ because at this point, $$y = x = mx$$ implies $$x(1 - m) = 0$$, so $$x = 0$$.

The area of the triangle can be found using the formula for the area of a triangle with vertices at $$(x_1, y_1)$$, $$(x_2, y_2)$$, and $$(x_3, y_3)$$:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \right| $$

Substituting the coordinates of the vertices $$(0, 0)$$, $$(2, 2)$$, and $$(2, 2m)$$:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 0(2 - 2m) + 2(2m - 0) + 2(0 - 2) \right| $$

Simplifying inside the absolute value:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 2(2m) + 2(-2) \right| $$

$$ = \frac{1}{2} \left| 4m - 4 \right| $$

Since we are given that the area is 3, we set up the equation:

$$ \frac{1}{2} \left| 4m - 4 \right| = 3 $$

Multiplying both sides by 2 to eliminate the fraction:

$$ \left| 4m - 4 \right| = 6 $$

This absolute value equation splits into two cases:

1. $$4m - 4 = 6$$

2. $$4m - 4 = -6$$

Solving the first case:

$$ 4m - 4 = 6 $$

$$ 4m = 10 $$

$$ m = \frac{5}{2} $$

Since this is positive, it does not satisfy $$m < 0$$.

Solving the second case:

$$ 4m - 4 = -6 $$

$$ 4m = -2 $$

$$ m = -\frac{1}{2} $$

This value is negative and satisfies $$m < 0$$.

Therefore, the correct value of $$m$$ is

$$ m = -\frac{1}{2} $$

The correct option is $$- \frac{1}{2}$$.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 3 September 2023
questions.question: What is the area between the curve f(x) = x |x| and x-axis for x = [-1, 1]?
questions.solution:
To find the area between the curve $ f(x) = x |x| $ and the x-axis for $ x $ in the interval $[-1, 1]$, we need to evaluate the definite integral of $ |x| \cdot x $ from $-1$ to $1$:

$$ \int_{-1}^{1} f(x) \, dx = \int_{-1}^{1} x|x| \, dx $$

Breaking it down into two integrals over the intervals $[-1, 0]$ and $[0, 1]$:

For $x \in [-1, 0]$, $ x |x| = x^2 $ because $ |x| = -x $:

$$ \int_{-1}^{0} x|x| \, dx = \int_{-1}^{0} x^2 \, dx $$

For $x \in [0, 1]$, $ x |x| = x^2 $ because $ |x| = x $:

$$ \int_{0}^{1} x|x| \, dx = \int_{0}^{1} x^2 \, dx $$

Calculate each integral:

$$ \int_{-1}^{0} x^2 \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{-1}^{0} = \left( \frac{0^3}{3} - \frac{(-1)^3}{3} \right) = 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{3} $$

$$ \int_{0}^{1} x^2 \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{1} = \left( \frac{1^3}{3} - \frac{0^3}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{3} - 0 = \frac{1}{3} $$

Add the results:

$$ \int_{-1}^{1} x|x| \, dx = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} $$

Hence, the area between the curve $ f(x) = x |x| $ and the x-axis for $ x $ in the interval $[-1, 1]$ is:

$$ \boxed{\frac{2}{3}} $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question: Which of the following statements does not form a part of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom?
options:
The position and velocity of the electrons in the orbit cannot be determined simultaneously.
The electron in the orbit nearest the nucleus has the lowest energy.
Electrons revolve in different orbits around the nucleus.
Energy of the electrons in the orbit is quantised.
questions.solution:
Bohr's model of the hydrogen atom primarily describes the behavior of electrons in fixed orbits around the nucleus with quantized energy levels. Below is an analysis of each statement:

Option A: The position and velocity of the electrons in the orbit cannot be determined simultaneously.

This statement refers to the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle and is not a part of Bohr's model. The Bohr model assumes electrons have well-defined orbits with known velocities and positions.

Option B: The electron in the orbit nearest the nucleus has the lowest energy.

This is indeed a part of Bohr's model. Bohr postulated that electrons occupy specific energy levels, and the closest orbit to the nucleus has the least energy.

Option C: Electrons revolve in different orbits around the nucleus.

This statement is a fundamental aspect of Bohr's model, where electrons move in discrete orbits or energy levels around the nucleus.

Option D: Energy of the electrons in the orbit is quantised.

This statement is also a key component of Bohr's model. Bohr proposed that the energy of electrons in these orbits is quantized, meaning electrons can only occupy certain energy levels.

Given the analysis, the statement that does not form a part of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom is:

Option A: The position and velocity of the electrons in the orbit cannot be determined simultaneously.

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question: The total energy of an electron in the second excited state of hydrogen atom is about $$-1.51 \mathrm{~eV}$$. Its kinetic energy in this state is
questions.solution:
Potential energy of an electron

$$(U)=-2 \text { (kinetic Energy K) }$$

So, total energy, $$E=U+K=-2 K+K$$

$$\begin{aligned} E & =-K \\ \Rightarrow K & =-E \Rightarrow K=-(-1.51 \mathrm{~eV})=1.51 \mathrm{~eV} \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: BITSAT 2023
questions.question: After two hours one-eight of the starting amount of a certain radioactive isotope remained undecayed. The half-life of the isotope is
questions.solution:
To determine the half-life of the radioactive isotope, we need to use the information given and the formula for radioactive decay. The formula for radioactive decay is:

$$N(t) = N_0 \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{t}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

where:

  • $N(t)$ is the remaining quantity of the isotope at time $t$.
  • $N_0$ is the initial quantity of the isotope.
  • $T_{\frac{1}{2}}$ is the half-life of the isotope.
  • $t$ is the elapsed time.

According to the given information, after 2 hours (which is 120 minutes), one-eighth of the starting amount of the isotope remains undecayed. Therefore, if $\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = \frac{1}{8}$, we can write:

$$\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{t}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

Substituting the values we have:

$$\frac{1}{8} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{120}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

We know that $\frac{1}{8}$ can be written as $\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^3$, therefore:

$$\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^3 = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{120}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

By equating the exponents, we get:

$$3 = \frac{120}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}$$

Solving for $T_{\frac{1}{2}}$:

$$T_{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{120}{3} = 40 \ \text{minutes}$$

Thus, the half-life of the isotope is 40 minutes. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: 40 min

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2023
questions.question:
(a) (i) Differentiate between 'distance of closest approach' and 'impact parameter'.

(ii) Determine the distance of closest approach when an alpha particle of kinetic energy 3.95 $$\mathrm{MeV}$$ approaches a nucleus of $$Z=79$$, stops and reverses its directions.

OR

(b) (i) State three postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom.

(ii) Find the angular momentum of an electron revolving in the second orbit in Bohr's hydrogen atom.
questions.solution:
(a) (i) Difference between "distance of closest approach" and "impact parameter":

Distance of closest approach Impact Parameter
Distance of closest approach is the distance of a charged particle from the centre of the nucleus where the total kinetic energy of the charged particle gets converted into potential energy. Impact parameter is the perpendicular distance between the path of projected charged particles and centre of the nucleus.
$$r=\frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^2\right)}$$ $$b=\frac{Z e^2 \cot (\theta / 2)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^2\right)}$$

$$\text { (ii) Distance of closest approach }=r=\frac{Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 E}$$

$$\begin{aligned} Q & =79 \mathrm{e}=79 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C} \\ q & =2 \mathrm{e}=2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C} \\ E & =3.95 \mathrm{MeV} \\ & =3.95 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore r=\left(9 \times 10^9\right) \frac{\left(79 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right) \times\left(2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)}{3.95 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =576 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =5.76 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned}$$

OR

(b) (i) Postulates of Bohr's theory: Postulate 1: In an atom electrons are revolving around the nucleus in definite circular orbits. These orbits are called 'stationary orbits' and each orbit or shell possesses fixed energy. While revolving in these orbits electrons do not emit any radiation.

Postulate 2: Electrons can move only those permissible orbits where the angular momenta of electrons are integral multiples of $$\frac{h}{2 \pi}$$ where $$h$$ is the Planck's constant.

Postulate 3: Transition of electrons may occur from one stationary orbit to another. During such transition energy may be emitted or absorbed following the relation $$E_i-E_f=h v$$ (where $$E_i \sim E_f$$ is the difference of energies of the two stable orbits).

(ii) Angular momentum of electron revolving in $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ orbit in Bohr's Hydrogen atom:

Angular momentum $$=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}$$

Here, $$n=2$$,

$$\therefore$$ Angular momentum $$=2 \times \frac{h}{2 \pi}=\frac{h}{\pi}$$

questions.question: A hydrogen atom makes a transition from $$n=5$$ to $$n=1$$ orbit. The wavelength of photon emitted is $$\lambda$$. The wavelength of photon emitted when it makes a transition from $$n=5$$ to $$n=2$$ orbit is
questions.solution:
In 1st case

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{5^2}\right] \\ \text{or,} \quad & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[1-\frac{1}{25}\right]=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{24}{25}\right] \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{R}=\frac{25}{24} \times \frac{1}{\lambda} \end{aligned}$$

In 2nd case

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & \frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}} =\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{5^2}\right] \\ \text { or, } & \frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}} =\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{25}\right]=\mathrm{R} \times \frac{21}{100} \\ \text { or, } & \frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}} =\frac{25}{24} \times \frac{1}{\lambda} \times \frac{21}{100} \\ \text { or, } & \frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}} =\frac{21}{96 \lambda} \\ \therefore & \lambda^{\prime} =\frac{96}{21} \lambda=\frac{32}{7} \lambda \end{array}$$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Outside Delhi Set 1 - 2023
questions.question:
(a) What is meant by ionisation energy? Write its value for hydrogen atom.

OR

(b) Define the term, mass defect. How is it related to stability of the nucleus?
questions.solution:
(a) Ionisation energy:

Ionisation energy is the minimum amount of energy which is to be supplied to an atom in its ground state so that it gets converted into an ion i.e., the minimum energy requires to shift an electron from $$n=1$$ to $$n=\infty$$.

The value of ionization energy for Hydrogen is 13.6 $$\mathrm{eV}$$.

OR

(b) Mass defect:

Mass defect is the difference of total mass of all the nucleons present in a nucleus and the rest mass of the nucleus.

Mass defect $$=\Delta m=Z m_P+(A-Z) m_n-M$$

As the mass defect increases, binding energy of the nucleus increases. This means the stability of the nucleus increases as mass defect increases.

questions.question:
(a) (i) Distinguish between nuclear fission and fusion giving an example of each.

(ii) Explain the release of energy in nuclear fission and fusion on the basis of binding energy per nucleon curve.

OR

(b) (i) How is the size of a nucleus found experimentally? Write the relation between the radius and mass number of a nucleus.

(ii) Prove that the density of a nucleus is independent of its mass number.
questions.solution:
(a) (i) Nuclear fission & fusion:

Nuclear fission Nuclear fusion
Heavy nucleus splits into comparatively lighter nuclei. Lighter nuclei fuse to form comparatively heavier nuclei.
Energy released is less than that of fusion. Energy released is more than that of fission.
Does not naturally occur. Occur naturally in stars.
Comparatively less amount of energy required to start the process. Huge amount of energy is required to start the process.
Application: Atomic bomb Application: Hydrogen bomb
Example:
$$\begin{aligned}
& { }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{238}+{ }_0 \mathrm{n}^1 \rightarrow{ }_{56} \mathrm{Ba}^{141}+ { }_{36} \mathrm{Kr}^{92}+3{ }_0 \mathrm{n}^1+\mathrm{Q}
\end{aligned}$$		 Example:
$$\begin{aligned}
& { }_1 \mathrm{H}^1+{ }_1 \mathrm{H}^1 \rightarrow{ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2+{ }_{+1} \mathrm{e}^0 +v+\mathrm{Q}
\end{aligned}$$

(ii) In nuclear fusion, the binding energy of the products is greater than the binding energy of reactants.

In nuclear fission, binding energy of fragments is greater than the binding energy of the parent.

This difference in binding energy is released in form of energy.

OR

(b) (i) Experimental determination of size of nucleus :

Size of nucleus was determined experimentally by Rutherford by his alpha particle scattering experiment.

In the experiment, a gold foil of thickness $$2.1 \times 10^{-7}$$ was bombarded by energetic $$\alpha$$-particles generated from $${ }_{83} \mathrm{Bi}^{214}$$ source.

Scattered $$\alpha$$-particles were observed on a $$\mathrm{ZnS}$$ screen with the help of a microscope.

It was observed that most of the $$\alpha$$-particles passed through the gold foil undeviated.

About $$14 \% ~\alpha$$-particles were scattered by an angle more than $$1^0$$.

1 out of $$8000 ~\alpha$$-particles was scattered by an angle more than $$90^{\circ}$$.

Very few $$\alpha$$-particles was scattered by an angle $$180^{\circ}$$.

From these observations Rutherford calculated the impact parameter and distance of closest approach and concluded that the size of nucleus lies between $$10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}$$ and $$10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}$$.

Relation between radius and mass number: $$\left.r=\mathrm{R}_0 \mathrm{A}^{1 / 3} \text { (where } R_0=1.25 \mathrm{fm}\right)$$

(ii) Density of nucleus is independent of mass number :

$$\begin{aligned} & \rho=\frac{M}{V} \\ \text{or,} \quad & \rho=\frac{m A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3} \end{aligned}$$

or, $$\rho=\frac{m A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A} \quad\left(\text { since } r=R_0 A^{1 / 3}\right)$$

$$\therefore \quad \rho=\frac{m}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3}$$

Hence, it is independent of mass number.

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2023 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
List-I shows different radioactive decay processes and List-II provides possible emitted particles. Match each entry in List-I with an appropriate entry from List-II, and choose the correct option.

List - I List - II
(P) ${ }_{92}^{238} U \rightarrow{ }_{91}^{234} \mathrm{~Pa}$ (1) one $\alpha$ particle and one $\beta^{+}$particle
(Q) ${ }_{82}^{214} \mathrm{~Pb} \rightarrow{ }_{82}^{210} \mathrm{~Pb}$ (2) three $\beta^{-}$particles and one $\alpha$ particle
(R) ${ }_{81}^{210} \mathrm{Tl} \rightarrow{ }_{82}^{206} \mathrm{~Pb}$ (3) two $\beta^{-}$particles and one $\alpha$ particle
(S) ${ }_{91}^{228} \mathrm{~Pa} \rightarrow{ }_{88}^{224} \mathrm{Ra}$ (4) one $\alpha$ particle and one $\beta^{-}$particle
(5) one $\alpha$ particle and two $\beta^{+}$particles
questions.solution:
In the given nuclear reaction :

$${}_{{Z_1}}{Z^{{A_1}}} \to {}_{{Z_2}}{Y^{{A_2}}} + {N_1}\,\,\left( {{}_2{H^4}} \right) + {N_2}\,\,\left( {{}_1{e^0}} \right) + {N_3}\,\,\left( {{}_{ - 1}{e^0}} \right)$$

we have the conservation of charge expressed as :

$ Z_1 = Z_2 + 2N_{1} + N_{2} - N_{3} $ ...............(i)

and the conservation of nucleons (mass number) is given by :

$ \begin{aligned} A_1 &= A_2 + 4N_{1} \\\\ N_{1} &= \frac{A_1 - A_2}{4} ..........(ii) \end{aligned} $

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$ \mathrm{N}_2-\mathrm{N}_3=\mathrm{Z}_1-\mathrm{Z}_2-\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}_1-\mathrm{A}_2}{2}\right) $$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { (P) }{ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{238} \rightarrow{ }_{91} \mathrm{~Pa}^{234} \\\\ & \mathrm{~N}_1=\frac{238-234}{4}=1 \rightarrow 1 \alpha \\\\ & \mathrm{N}_2-\mathrm{N}_3=(92-91)-\left(\frac{4}{2}\right)=-1 \rightarrow 1 \beta^{-}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { (Q) }{ }_{82} \mathrm{~Pb}^{214} \rightarrow { }_{82} \mathrm{~Pb}^{210} \\\\ & \quad \mathrm{~N}_1=\frac{214-210}{4}=1 \rightarrow 1 \alpha \\\\ & \mathrm{N}_2-\mathrm{N}_3=(82-82)-\left(\frac{4}{2}\right)=-2 \rightarrow 2 \beta^{-}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & (\mathrm{R}){ }_{81} \mathrm{~T} \ell^{210} \rightarrow { }_{82} \mathrm{~Pb}^{206} \\\\ & \mathrm{~N}_1=\frac{210-206}{4}=1 \rightarrow 1 \alpha \\\\ & \mathrm{N}_2-\mathrm{N}_3=(81-83)-\frac{4}{2}=-3 \rightarrow 3 \beta^{-}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { (S) }{ }_{91} \mathrm{~Pa}^{228} \rightarrow_{88} \mathrm{Ra}^{224} \\\\ & \mathrm{~N}_1=\frac{228-224}{4}=1 \rightarrow 1 \alpha \\\\ & \mathrm{N}_2-\mathrm{N}_3=(91-88)-\frac{4}{2}=1 \beta^{+}\end{aligned}$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2023 Paper 2 Online
questions.question: In a radioactive decay process, the activity is defined as $A=-\frac{d N}{d t}$, where $N(t)$ is the number of radioactive nuclei at time $t$. Two radioactive sources, $S_1$ and $S_2$ have same activity at time $t=0$. At a later time, the activities of $S_1$ and $S_2$ are $A_1$ and $A_2$, respectively. When $S_1$ and $S_2$ have just completed their $3^{\text {rd }}$ and $7^{\text {th }}$ half-lives, respectively, the ratio $A_1 / A_2$ is _________.
questions.solution:
$$ A_1=A_0 e^{-\lambda_1 t_1} $$

also $A_2=A_0 e^{-\lambda_2 t_2}$

At $t_1=\frac{3 \ln 2}{\lambda_1}, $

$A_1=A_0 e^{-\lambda_1 \frac{3 \ln 2}{\lambda_1}}$

$$ =A_0 e^{-3 \ln 2} $$ ...........(i)

Similarly, at

$$ \begin{aligned} &t_2= \frac{7 \ln 2}{\lambda_2}, \\\\& A_2=A_0 e^{-\lambda_2 \frac{7 \ln 2}{\lambda_2}} \\\\ & =A_0 e^{-7 \ln 2} ...........(ii) \end{aligned} $$

From (i) and (ii)

$$ \frac{A_1}{A_2}=\frac{A_0 e^{-3 \ln 2}}{A_0 e^{-7 \ln 2}}=\frac{2^{-3}}{2^{-7}}=\frac{1}{2^{-4}}=2^4=16 $$

$\therefore \frac{A_1}{A_2}=16$
